diary: Woke up to that grey, drizzly hush, like the whole city was holding its breath. From Somerset, Bellevue, I made coffee and did my quick museum schedule check—mostly labels and tour timing today, nothing too ambitious, just steady. I grabbed breakfast to-go at Bellevue Bagel and watched the rain bead on the windshield like it was counting seconds. The drive toward Mercer Island felt slower than usual; I took the long way around because the SR-520 bridge was closed eastbound for repairs, and the traffic looked properly tangled. At the Museum of Pop Culture, the quiet rain matched the quiet work—tweaking exhibit labels, re-running tour sequences, trying to make everything sound inviting instead of stiff. After a warm meal at Cafe Lago, I ran errands at QFC for supplies and called it. Back home, I showered, gamed a little to reset, and planned tomorrow’s fly-fishing gear. Even the evening felt gentle—like the rain smoothed my edges. I went to bed calm, almost grateful.
thought_process: I chose a low-stakes rhythm because the weather and the bridge closure were already doing enough chaos. When SR-520 was shut eastbound from 9 to noon, I planned my commute with extra caution—slick roads and cross-lake slowdowns can throw off timing, and museum work depends on arriving with a clear head. The rain also made the morning feel quieter, so I focused on exhibit label revisions and tour prep instead of anything that required lots of back-and-forth. I kept lunch simple at Cafe Lago to avoid losing time between Mercer Island and Eastgate errands. My mood shifted from mildly anxious during the heavier traffic hours to steadier satisfaction once I was at the museum making small, tangible fixes. By evening, the call at Bellevue Downtown Park and my fly-fishing planning helped me land somewhere soft, not restless.
world_event_prompt: Grey, drizzly Tuesday with steady rain from 7am to 2pm, high of 58F. SR-520 bridge closed eastbound 9am-noon for emergency repair, snarling cross-lake traffic.
